7.10 (parallel) has ``haywâlon'', but also ``gĕlis prih lajun''. ModJ ṅabĕrake, ``to slacken pace''. Cf KBW s.v. limut, where ``abĕr de nira lumaku'' is the rendering of ``arubĕd dera lumampah''; and KBW s.v. gere: kidaṅ muni acĕluk kady aṅabĕrakĕn laku, kady ataken lampah ipun (Stn). Gonda (BKI 88.485), referring to Kern (in KK 57.13), proposes ``quickly running away or about''. Then Wir 58.4 aṅabĕri would be ``to quicken one's pace''.

definition: (Skt) beginning, commencement; first, principal, chief; excellent, exquisite, fine, eminent. As final part of a (Skt) compound: beginning with, and so on. As first part of a compound: first, primeval. In these cases it is often difficult to decide between the reading ādi and adhi (above, over and above). The orthography of the MSS regarding d, dh and (in prose and because of sandhi in most of the kakawin-loci) regarding a, ā is uncertain, while there may be little difference in meaning (ādidewa, first god; adhidewa, supreme god, presiding deity; ādirāja, first king; adhirāja, supreme king, emperor). Instances of unambiguous readings: adhimukhya (BK 2.31); ādikawya (BK 1.25); kīrti saṅ ādisajjana (Nag 80.3). 1. beginning, commencement. See also anādi. 2. the first, he who begins, the principal or chief, what is in the first place 3. (as final part of a compound) beginning with, and so on 4. excellent, exquisite, fine, eminent (frequent in later texts)

(Skt) 1. the uppermost or foremost part, top, summit, point. See also daitya-, krūra-, kuśa-, kuṭa-, mahāpaṇḍita-, nakha-, nāsikā-, parwata-, purohita-, sañjata-, śilā-, śūla-, suta-, tīkṣṇa-, tomara-, uttama-, wipra-. 2. prominent, first, chief, best. definition: (Skt form, figure, shape, stature, appearance, external gesture or aspect of the body, expression of the face) 1. form, appearance, aspect. It is also used where there is no question of a physically perceptible form, in the wider sense of ``state''. See also dhūpa-, durga-, kṛṣṇa-, krūra-, māyā-, megha-, mudra-, mukha-, nir-, piṇḍa-, rakta-, rudra-, rukma-, sa-, siṅha-, sthūla-, śuddha-, sūkṣma-, śūnya-, śweta-, tad-, ūrṇa-, warṇa-. 2. facial expression, the outward sign of inward feelings 3. interval, space, number, measure; distance, length of time; to the number of, at the time of, for the length of, at a distance of. It is a meaning which ākāra in Skt does not appear to have. 4. countless?

definition: (Skt) unbroken; unhusked grains. We find it very often in combination with gandha: gandhâkṣata, but the fact that akṣata also occurs separately makes the derivation from Skt gandhāṣṭaka, a mixture of eight fragrant substances, or from Skt gandhakā#ṣṭha, a fragrant wood (sandal etc) less probable.

sub_entries.definition: a certain bird of prey. The texts suggest a large creature, which can attack a man; an eagle?

citations.text: 15.57: inibĕrakĕn śīghra kadi rūpa niṅṅ alap-alap;
19.73: tulyanyâlap-alap gĕlap juga ya riṅ śīghra;
23.46: Pawanātmajâdbhuta alāp-alap juga r alap gadānya tinikĕl;
(?:) 24.109: nityâniṅtiṅ maniṅtiṅ riṅ alap-alapa riṅ lūṅan lagi maliṅ (or alap-alap ariṅ lūṅha n lagi maliṅ?);
25.65: (see under alapan);
34.31: krora panūbnya sāhasa baṅunn alap-alap alapā;
67.205 f. (907) B.14: maṅliṅai ko riṅ tĕgal tan samwa (read samwar = sambĕr) ko ṅ uluṅ-uluṅ, tan sikĕp ko ṅ alap-alap; = OJO 102, a.15. Unclear:
81.37: nyâlap-alapnya kālap aṅalan (read aṅalap?) manah ikaṅ aṅalap (alap-alap: ``what is for the taking, snacks''?).
22.64: jrih taṅ wānara ... / tātan rākṣasa paṅhiḍĕpnya alapan ta ya juga sakala;
47.11: abhimāna tulya halapan.

22.64, with ``more frightful than a rākṣasa'' and ``sakala'' suggests a monster or Kāla rather than a bird (see alap-alap). Perhaps it is ``the robber, seizer'' (of the soul, of life); cf RY 19.49: kadi pwa yamakiṅka#râṅalapi jīwa niṅ rākṣasa. Interestingly, the bal. com. for AW 16.8 and 25.11 (see under alap-alapan) has: kāla mṛtyu. In the following it could then be a qualification of alap#alap: RY 25.65: mandĕl daryas wuru-wuru alap-alap alapan.

citations.text: 41.5: Jitākṣa maṅsö alu ghora sañjata;

41.6: tĕkĕl maparwâṅ alu ghora tan bisa. GR alugora, name of a battle-club (in Wayaṅ-texts); alu-alu, name of a weapon, now unknown, prob. a kind of battle-club. Alu, pestle, is common in several Indonesian languages: jav. mal. sund. (alu; halu) bal. (hĕlu), sas. (anak lu), tag. (halo), bat. (andalu, lalu). Is it of Indonesian origin? But cf Skt ayogra (MW pestle, lex.); ghora (MW: a kind of mythical weapon, Mah. 5.3491).
gives a passage from Canṭakaparwa: saṅ Kṛṣṇa hastanya mijil cakra, saṅ Kakārṣaṇa mijil ghora sakeṅ karatala.

definition: 1. what? (interrogative pronoun) 2. how could ... ; why?; also simply to introduce a question (as in ModJ) 3. Apa by itself or in its derived forms also occurs as an indefinite pronoun, ``something (or other)'', but nearly always in negative sentences. No example has been found of a form apa-apa, common in ModJ. Without a negative:

definition: for, as, because. The n is the connective particle and can be replaced by the particles connoting the first (apak), second (apat, apad) or third (apar) person, and can drop when tan follows (apa tan). See also kunaṅ apan,- tâpan,- pwâpan,- nyâpan (s.v. kunaṅ) and kintu tâpan (s.v. kintu).

definition: (Skt apsaras, apsarā, ``going in the waters or between the waters of the clouds'', class of female divinities (sometimes called ``nymphs''); they inhabit the sky, but often visit the earth; wives of the gandharwas; have the faculty of changing their shapes at will) In OJ apsara is the male, apsarī the female. There is no evidence that an apsarī is considered the wife of a gandharwa.

definition: despondent, dejected, down-heart#ed. Skt. āśā means ``wish, desire, hope, expectation,'' which is perhaps to be seen in OJ salah-asa and Mal. putus asa. But Skt verb āśaṃs has another meaning, viz. ``to suspect, fear'', although of this nothing can be found under āśā or its compounds. See also pra#labdha-.

definition: (Skt) soul, principle of life, life. It dwells in the body, which it leaves temporarily (cf tan pātma) or definitely in death to go to the deity (supreme soul) with which it is essentially one. After death it is considered as a subtle body. See also antara-, cetana-, jita-, jīwa-, mahā-, mantra-, nir-, para-, parama-, Śiwa-, wāhya-.

definition: (Skt looking upon, viewing; awalokana, seeing, beholding, viewing; awalokana, seeing, beholding, viewing, observing; look, glance). We find in OJ awaloka, walokana, awalokana, mawalokana. Walo#kana is a substantive, the other forms are used adjectivally. It is hence possible that waloka and walokana were regarded as base#words. The meaning seems to be: with clear insight, free from doubt, (perhaps even:) quiet, untroubled.
